data.title: Cans gone wild: High Noon recall after vodka sneaks into Celsius cans
data.description:
High Noon is recalling certain 12-packs of its vodka seltzers due to a mislabeling issue involving Celsius (NASDAQ:CELH) energy drinks.

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration issued a recall order  for High Noon beach variety packs that include cans of vodka-infused beverages mislabeled as CELSIUS ASTRO VIBE energy drinks. Therefore, consumers who believe they are drinking an alcohol-free energy drink may end up with a can of vodka seltzer with an alcohol content of 4.5%.  

The issue likely resulted from a shared third-party packaging supplier for both High Noon and Celsius (NASDAQ:CELH) who likely shipped empty cans of CELSIUS ASTRO VIBE to High Noon’s production facility. The cans were then filled with vodka seltzer and packaged into High Noon’s beach variety packs.

The impacted packs were sent to distributors in Florida, Michigan, New York, Ohio, Oklahoma, South Carolina, Virginia and Wisconsin between July 21 and July 23.

Celsius (NASDAQ:CELH) shares were drifting lower on the heels of the recall notice, although still holding onto a 1% gain on the day.

High Noon is made by privately-owned E & J Gallo Winery.

data.title: National Fuel Gas outlines 6% production growth and $8–$8.50 EPS target for fiscal 2026 while advancing pipeline projects
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: National Fuel Gas Company (NFG) Q3 2025

Management View

  • David P. Bauer, President and CEO, highlighted, "National Fuel had an excellent third quarter. We're seeing great execution across the company, and our momentum continues to build." Bauer emphasized Seneca's Eastern development area's strong performance, noting, "Production for the quarter was up 16% from last year." He announced updated full-year production guidance, expecting approximately 8% growth versus fiscal 2024, and introduced fiscal 2026 guidance with a production target of 440 to 455 Bcf, a 6% projected increase at the midpoint, achieved with 4% less capital.
  • Bauer also pointed to significant pipeline expansion opportunities, referencing the Shippingport Lateral Project and the Tioga Pathway Project. He stated, "We announced our Shippingport Lateral Project... designed to deliver a significant portion of the natural gas supply for the Shippingport power station and a new co-located data center," with a preceding agreement for 205,000 dekatherms per day of capacity starting in Q4 2026. Combined, these projects are expected to generate north of $30 million in new annual revenue.
  • On capital returns, Bauer explained, "In June, we raised our dividend for the 55th consecutive year to an annual rate of $2.14 per share," and addressed the paused buyback program, citing a focus on potential growth opportunities.
  • Timothy J. Silverstein, CFO, stated, "We had a great third quarter with adjusted operating results increasing 66% versus last year. The main drivers were higher natural gas prices, lower per unit operating costs at Seneca and continued growth in production and gathering throughput."
  • Justin I. Loweth, President of Seneca Resources and National Fuel Midstream, reported, "Production increased 6% from the prior quarter to 112 Bcf, driving gathering throughput to a new quarterly high of 133 Bcf."

Outlook

  • Silverstein narrowed fiscal 2025 earnings guidance to a range of $6.80 to $6.95 per share, noting a reduction in NYMEX gas price forecast for the fourth quarter from $3.50 to $3.25. For fiscal 2026, he provided preliminary EPS guidance: "Using a $4 price, which closely approximates the current strip, we'd expect earnings to be in a range of $8 to $8.50 per share. At the midpoint, this reflects a 20% increase from fiscal 2025."
  • Seneca's production guidance for fiscal 2026 is 440 to 455 Bcf, a 6% increase at the midpoint, with expected capital expenditures 4% lower than fiscal 2025. The company anticipates a 5% to 6% increase in utility customer margin next year and expects utility O&M to increase by approximately 5%.

Financial Results

  • Silverstein reported, "Adjusted operating results increasing 66% versus last year." Production for the quarter reached 112 Bcf, up 6% sequentially, and gathering throughput set a new quarterly high of 133 Bcf. Dividend was raised to $2.14 per share.
  • Capital expenditure guidance for fiscal 2025 was tightened to a range of $500 million to $510 million, with expectations for a meaningful step-up in fourth quarter spending. For fiscal 2026, capital spending is forecasted at $470 million to $500 million, a $20 million or 4% reduction relative to the midpoint of 2025 guidance.

Q&A

  • Benjamin Zachary Parham, JPMorgan, asked about the paused buyback. Bauer responded, "It's entirely driven by our capital allocation priorities... Of late, we've been looking at different opportunities. And as you said, want to keep balance sheet flexibility."
  • Parham followed up on cash taxes. Silverstein answered, "In the near term, it's probably, say, in the 200 to 300 basis point area. So... next year, we'll be in the sort of low to mid-single-digit cash tax rate."
  • Margaret Ellen Drefke, Goldman Sachs, inquired about Tioga Pathway project spending cadence. Bauer explained, "We'll kick off construction in the spring... bulk of the spending will be in the summer on the contractors and installing the lines."
  • Drefke asked about service cost trends. Loweth responded, "We're really not seeing a lot of inflationary pressure on the steel side... I'd say slightly down to neutral is kind of how we think about the world broadly across the services."
  • Noah B. Hungness, BofA, questioned supply agreement positioning for Northeast Pennsylvania. Loweth stated, "We're excited about those opportunities, and we've got lots of active dialogue... we've got all the pieces to find ways to participate in that."
  • Hungness asked about upside risk to Gen 3 well productivity. Loweth said, "Those last 2 pads have been excellent... leading-edge wells are a bit better than our type curve, and we'll look to continue that performance."
  • John Christopher Freeman, Raymond James, probed pipeline project benefits. Loweth replied, "NESE does have some pretty meaningful, we think, positive implications if it moves forward... That could not only free up the benefits we have on our existing FT, but also create an opportunity to have more firm sales."
  • Timothy Michael Winter, Gabelli, asked about growth opportunities and regulated pipeline investments. Bauer replied, "Our top priority would be growing organically... shipping port and Tioga Pathways are certainly great projects. We call those the singles and doubles type projects that over time add up to a lot of growth for the company."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed management on capital allocation, productivity, and growth project cadence, maintaining a neutral to slightly positive tone focused on execution and strategic clarity.
  • Management tone was confident and forward-looking during prepared remarks, backed by language such as "we are well positioned" and "we expect to build on that momentum," while responses in Q&A remained composed and methodical, with Bauer stressing capital discipline and flexibility.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the management's tone remains confident, though more emphasis is placed on operational execution and capital efficiency. Analyst sentiment continues to be constructive, pressing for specifics on project cadence and capital priorities but not displaying skepticism.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for fiscal 2026 is more detailed, with explicit EPS and production targets, whereas the previous quarter focused on fiscal 2025 and the broader multi-year growth trajectory.
  • The company is now emphasizing the Shippingport Lateral Project and Tioga Pathway Project, identifying near-term revenue and growth opportunities, while continuing to highlight capital efficiency improvements.
  • Analyst focus remains on capital allocation, productivity gains, and pipeline expansion, but this quarter saw more discussion of project cadence, supply agreements, and cost trends.
  • Management tone is consistently confident, with ongoing stress on capital discipline and operational efficiency. Analyst tone shows continued interest in the specifics of growth projects and productivity enhancements.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited potential challenges from utility customer receivables in arrears and the need to accelerate write-offs due to state policy impacts during the pandemic, though a tracker mechanism permits deferral and recovery of excess write-offs.
  • The company acknowledged the risk of higher O&M costs due to union contract extensions and ongoing negotiations in 2026, as well as general inflationary trends.
  • Regulatory and permitting risk remains a key concern for large-scale pipeline projects, with Bauer noting, "I think we're going to need permitting reform before you see a lot of really big projects get built."
  • Analysts raised questions about capital allocation flexibility, service cost trends, and productivity estimates, but management indicated robust hedging, cost controls, and well inventory as mitigating factors.

Final Takeaway

National Fuel Gas Company’s third quarter call delivered a clear message of disciplined capital allocation, operational excellence, and targeted growth. Management is focused on driving production and capital efficiency, with explicit fiscal 2026 guidance projecting a 6% increase in production and EPS of $8 to $8.50 per share, alongside a robust pipeline expansion strategy and prudent capital returns. The company’s deep well inventory, strong hedge book, and integrated midstream assets are positioned to capture value as demand and infrastructure opportunities accelerate, supporting a positive outlook for shareholder value creation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: BGC projects up to $765M Q3 revenue following record Q2 driven by ECS and FMX growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: BGC Group, Inc. (BGC) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Sean A. Windeatt, Co-Chief Executive Officer, reported "historic results, generating record revenues of $784 million, a 42% increase versus last year." He highlighted that "BGC is now the world's largest ECS broker," and noted that FMX had "its best ever quarter with record volumes and market share across both FMX UST and FX platforms." Windeatt announced the launch of a cost reduction program, which is expected "to be completed by year-end and deliver at least $25 million in annualized savings through expense synergies." He stated these savings "will enhance our profitability, drive margins higher, and we expect them to deliver long-term shareholder value."

  • John Joseph Abularrage, Co-CEO, detailed that ECS revenues grew by 122.2% to $261.6 million, while rates revenues increased by 20.8% to $200.6 million. He highlighted "FMX UST generated record average daily volume of $68 billion in the second quarter, a 45% increase compared to last year" and that FMX FX "nearly doubled its ADV to a record $15.6 billion."

  • Jason Williams Hauf, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "BGC generated second quarter revenues of $784 million, reflecting growth across all of our geographies." He noted "pretax adjusted earnings grew by 38% to a record $173.6 million," and "post-tax adjusted earnings per share improved by 34.8% to an all-time high of $0.31 per share." Hauf added, "our liquidity was $965.9 million compared with $897.8 million at year-end 2024."

Outlook

  • Sean A. Windeatt provided third quarter 2025 guidance: "We expect to generate total revenues of between $715 million and $765 million as compared to $561.1 million in the third quarter of 2024, which at the midpoint of our guidance would represent approximately 32% revenue growth." Excluding OTC, third quarter revenues are expected to grow around 12% at the midpoint. He added, "We anticipate pretax adjusted earnings to be in the range of $150 million to $165 million versus $126.7 million last year." The full year 2025 adjusted earnings tax rate is expected between 10% and 12%.

Financial Results

  • The company reported record revenues of $784 million for Q2, with EMEA revenues up 50.3%, Americas up 40.3%, and Asia Pacific up 17.4%. Compensation and employee benefits increased by 53.1% under GAAP and 51.4% for adjusted earnings, attributed to the acquisition of OTC and higher commissionable revenues. Non-compensation expenses under GAAP rose by 30.5%. Adjusted EBITDA reached $213.3 million, up 31.4%. The company repurchased more than 16 million shares during the quarter, with the full impact to be seen in Q3.

Q&A

  • Patrick Malcolm Moley, Piper Sandler & Co.: "How do you view the growth algorithm from here? ... How should we think about the growth algorithm of the company and the margin trajectory from here?" Sean A. Windeatt responded that "if you take out the OTC acquisition, nothing changed, great gearing revenue growth of 21% and pretax AE at 26%, 27%. ... we've embarked on our cost reduction program, $25 million cost reduction program, which will have completed by the end of the year. So you'll see that benefit in 2026."

  • Moley: "Could you just elaborate on how you're feeling about [FMX futures] business, the traction you're seeing there and where you're at in terms of some of the FCM and partner onboard that you're still waiting for?" Jean-Pierre Aubin replied, "We are very, very happy where we are today. ... Our UST market share is 35%, and from 33% and 30% 2 quarters ago, success #1. ... As UST and software volume and open interest continue to scale, we do expect attention to shift to UST futures." John Joseph Abularrage added, "we're at the 9 that we had mentioned before. ... By year-end, we'll have 12 on, which will give us the vast majority of customer assets once that's done."

  • Elias Noah Abboud, BofA Securities: "Are there any additional complexities or challenges of treasury futures that you did not have to grapple with the SOFR that are worth highlighting?" Abularrage replied, "No is the answer. ... There's no additional impediments or speed bumps. ... you'll see U.S. treasury futures follow the success of SOFR."

  • Abboud: "Can you give us any insight into how many [FCMs] have open interest on the platform? ... What's the cause of the holdup?" Abularrage stated, "you can assume that all or nearly all are there and have open interest on the platform, and there is no holdup. It's just a matter of growing it as we go forward."

  • Abboud: "Can you help us make sense of some of those recent [FX business] trends?" Windeatt explained, "outside of equities and ECS ... clients have the choice to trade either voice or electronic. ... Our gut feeling is we're happy for our clients to trade in whichever method they choose. I would expect that trend to go slightly more electronic again over time now that the market has stabilized."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a positive to slightly positive tone, focusing on growth trajectory, integration progress, and FMX traction, while probing for clarity on margin improvement, integration milestones, and product mix trends.

  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and responses, emphasizing record performance, successful acquisition integration, and targeted cost reductions. Windeatt used phrases like "we've embarked on our cost reduction program ... completed by the end of the year" and "incredibly happy with both the voice hybrid and the FMX piece of the FX business."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management demonstrated increased confidence, with fewer defensive or hesitant statements and more emphasis on execution and growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q3 revenue increased to a range of $715 million to $765 million compared to the previous quarter's Q2 guidance of $715 million to $765 million, but the revenue base and growth rates are higher due to the OTC acquisition.

  • Strategic focus has shifted toward integration of OTC, realization of $25 million in annualized cost savings, and accelerated growth in ECS and FMX.

  • Analyst questions continued to focus on growth sustainability, integration progress, and product evolution, with increased attention to margin trajectory post-acquisition.

  • Key metrics such as ECS and FMX volumes, share repurchases, and margin expansion have moved higher versus the previous quarter.

  • Management's confidence has increased, emphasizing successful execution, market share gains, and cost synergy realization.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited risks tied to integration and realization of acquisition synergies, noting the cost reduction program will be completed by year-end.

  • No major operational impediments or delays were reported in FMX or ECS business lines; management noted that growth in FCM participation is progressing as planned.

  • Analyst concerns centered on the pace of margin improvement and the balance between voice and electronic trading platforms.

Final Takeaway

BGC Group delivered record second quarter results, driven by substantial growth in ECS and FMX platforms and successful integration of OTC. Management projects continued strong performance into the third quarter with revenue guidance between $715 million and $765 million and expects to realize at least $25 million in annualized cost savings by year-end. The company remains focused on expanding market share, improving margins, and capturing further opportunities in electronic trading and financial markets globally.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Radian signals $795M 2025 capital return target while strengthening mortgage insurance portfolio
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Radian Group Inc. (RDN) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Richard Gerald Thornberry highlighted "strong performance for Radian in the second quarter and the first half of the year," noting a net income of $142 million and a return on equity of 12.5%. Thornberry reported that "primary mortgage insurance in force...grew to another all-time high of $277 billion" and that "our mortgage insurance portfolio delivered strong credit performance with cures exceeding new defaults during the quarter." Thornberry stated, "Overall, our outlook for our Mortgage Insurance business remains positive."
  • Thornberry addressed the housing market, mentioning "housing supply constraints and elevated home prices," but pointed to "stability in the consumer and labor market, including positive employment trends and wage growth." He emphasized that Radian leverages "proprietary data and analytics, including our RADAR rates risk-based pricing to inform our strategic pricing decisions."
  • Thornberry noted, "The recent passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act further enhances this affordability as mortgage insurance premiums are once again tax deductible." He further stated, "We remain closely aligned with policymakers on Capitol Hill, the administration and the FHFA and our shared mission of bridging the gap to affordable, responsible and sustainable homeownership."
  • CFO Sumita Pandit reported, "We achieved net income of $142 million or $1.02 per diluted share, an increase compared to $0.98 per diluted share reported in the first quarter." Pandit highlighted a "return on equity of 12.5%" and a book value per share of $33.18, noting "this book value per share growth is in addition to our regular stockholder dividends, which were $35 million during the quarter." Pandit also commented that "our provision for losses and related credit trends continue to be positive with strong cure activity and very low claim levels."

Outlook

  • Pandit reiterated, "With strong persistency rates and the current positive industry pricing environment, we expect the in-force premium yield to generally remain stable for the remainder of the year as well."
  • She detailed, "As communicated previously, we expect operating expenses of $320 million for the full year 2025, a decrease of 8% compared to $348 million in 2024."
  • Thornberry stated, "This year, we expect to bring up $795 million from Radian Guaranty, of which this year, so far, we brought up $400 million. So we have good visibility to cash flow from Radian Guaranty kind of now into the future."

Financial Results

  • Radian reported total revenues of $318 million and net premiums earned of $234 million for the quarter. The mortgage insurance in force reached $277 billion. New insurance written for the quarter was $14.3 billion, up 3% year-over-year. Persistency rate was reported at 84%.
  • The in-force premium yield for the mortgage insurance portfolio remained at 38 basis points. Total defaults decreased to approximately 22,000 loans, with a portfolio default rate of 2.27%, down 6 basis points from the previous quarter. New defaults decreased 8% quarter-over-quarter. Net expense for losses was $12 million, compared to $15 million in the first quarter.
  • Adjusted pretax operating loss for All Other was approximately $16.4 million, primarily due to lower revenue in the Mortgage Conduit business from mark-to-market changes. Other operating expenses were $89 million, higher than the prior quarter due to the timing of annual share-based incentive grants.
  • Total return of capital to stockholders in the first half of the year exceeded $500 million, with 13.5 million shares repurchased. Holding company liquidity was $784 million at quarter end.

Q&A

  • Douglas Michael Harter, UBS: Asked about holding company liquidity and sustainability of capital return. Pandit responded, "We ended the quarter at $784 million, which is lower than the first quarter number. But again...we've used some of that liquidity towards opportunistic share repurchases." She added, "We would be comfortable saying that right now, our liquidity is quite in excess of what we may think is the appropriate buffer at the holding company."
  • Harter, UBS: Asked about sustainability of the $795 million dividend up to the holdco. Pandit stated, "The dividend from RGI is driven by the statutory net income of the prior year...our stat net income would be the best proxy of our dividend capacity from RGI to group."
  • Bose Thomas George, KBW: Inquired about the magnitude of marks on loans held for sale. Thornberry responded, "The pipeline and loans held for sale grow to...close to $900 million...the impact combined with kind of higher expenses with the higher volume was about $9 million in the quarter."
  • George, KBW: Asked about breakeven timeline for Home Genius and strategic options. Thornberry said, "We made a decision in the second quarter to discontinue kind of our investment in the technology on that business." He noted, "The volatility has come through our Conduit business," with Title and Real Estate businesses "generally consistent with the prior quarter."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone focused on liquidity management, capital return sustainability, and segment performance, with a neutral to slightly positive attitude, probing for clarity on recurring capital returns and the effect of market volatility, but not pressing aggressively.
  • Management's tone was confident regarding capital flexibility, credit performance, and operational execution. Thornberry emphasized, "We have good visibility to cash flow from Radian Guaranty kind of now into the future," and Pandit reiterated, "our liquidity is quite in excess of what we may think is the appropriate buffer."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained a steady tone, with management expressing continued confidence and analysts focusing on capital distribution and risk management.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Book value per share growth accelerated to 12% year-over-year from 11% in the prior quarter. Persistency rate moderated to 84% from 86%. Mortgage insurance in force grew to $277 billion from $274 billion. Net income this quarter was $142 million, slightly down from $145 million last quarter. Operating expenses increased to $89 million from $77 million, attributed to timing for share-based grants. New insurance written increased to $14.3 billion from $9.5 billion in Q1. Default rate improved to 2.27% from 2.33%.
  • Strategic focus shifted with the discontinuation of investment in Real Estate Tech (Home Genius) technology. Share repurchase activity ramped up, with 13.5 million shares bought back in the first half versus 6.5 million in Q1.
  • Management confidence and capital return messaging remained strong, similar to last quarter, while analysts continued to probe around capital deployment and business segment contributions.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "housing supply constraints and elevated home prices" as ongoing market challenges but noted stability in consumer and labor markets. The Conduit business experienced volatility, leading to a $9 million mark-to-market impact. Real Estate Tech investment was discontinued to reduce future drag. Operating expense increases were attributed to timing rather than structural cost escalation. Management underscored ongoing monitoring of liquidity and capital buffers and maintained a cautious approach to capital allocation decisions.

Final Takeaway

Radian’s second quarter underscored robust credit performance, growth in mortgage insurance in force, and a proactive capital management approach, with $795 million targeted for capital return in 2025. The company continued to optimize its portfolio, maintained stable premium yields, and demonstrated discipline in liquidity management and expense control. Discontinuation of investment in Real Estate Tech and significant share repurchases highlight a focus on long-term shareholder value. Management remains confident in Radian’s ability to navigate market volatility and deliver sustainable returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: NCR Atleos jumps as investor sees 270% upside
data.description:
NCR Atleos (NYSE:NATL) soared 15% after an investor forecast 270% upside for shares of the ATM and digital banking company to $100 a share. 

"We believe NATL is worth $100/share at 13x FY27 EPS of $7.7, 270% upside from current levels,"  investment firm Alta Fox wrote in a presentation released on Thursday. "With the taxable spin-off window now closed, we believe a strategic sale could become a potential outcome if a re-rating does not materialize in the medium term."

Alta Fox argues that if the company continues to deleverage its balance sheet and initiate share repurchases, the stock could re-rate as investors acknowledge NCR Atleos's earnings owner. 

The Alta Fox presentation comes as NCR Atleos shares had plunged 21% before today.

data.title: Gap looks to reinvigorate the Athleta brand with the key hiring of a longtime Nike executive
data.description:
Gap (GPS) stirred things up in the retail sector this week with the appointment of longtime Nike (NKE) executive Maggie Gauger to be the new Global Brand President and CEO of Athleta.

Gauger succeeds Chris Blakeslee, who led the company through the first stages of its turnaround over the last two years and will remain in an advisory role to ease the transition. Gauger boasts more than 20 years of experience at Nike (NKE), most recently leading its North America Women’s Business, and has an extensive background in retail, strategy, and digital commerce.

Gap (NYSE:GAP) pointed to Gauger's expertise in business transformation, consumer-centric strategy, and her commitment to empowering women and girls—qualities, which are seen as vital for Athleta's new direction as it looks to regain a competitive edge in the women’s activewear market.

Gauger takes over the Athleta brand on August 1. 

Athleta struggled in fiscal 2024, with the brand reporting a 1% decrease in sales to $1.4 billion. During Q1 of this year, sales rose 2% and comparable sales were up 5% as Athleta’s new product and marketing strategies gained some traction.

Athleta was founded in 1998 in Petaluma, California, as a women-focused athletic and lifestyle apparel brand. In 2008, the brand was acquired by Gap (NYSE:GAP), and its first standalone retail store opened in 2011. Athleta now operates over 250 stores across the United States and Canada.

data.title: Goldman Sachs's Solomon to meet with Trump - report
data.description:
President Donald Trump is scheduled to meet with Goldman Sachs (NYSE:GS) Chairman and CEO David Solomon on Thursday afternoon, according to a media report, as investors and economists are still trying to get a handle on the scope and potential impact of the administration's tariff policy.

Solomon and Trump are set to meet at the White House on Thursday, Bloomberg News reported, citing people familiar with the matter. One of the people said the topics on tap include the economy and geopolitics.

The meeting comes after JPMorgan Chase (JPM) CEO Jamie Dimon reportedly met with Trump last week to discuss the economy, trade and affordable housing.

With deregulation among the Trump administration's priorities, bank executives have been vocal about what they'd like to see, especially in terms of adjusting capital ratio requirements. Last week, bank executives and lobbyists gathered at the Federal Reserve's  Integrated Review of the Capital Framework for Large Banks Conference in Washington, DC.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: FirstEnergy outlines 20% potential CapEx increase amid surging data center demand
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: FirstEnergy Corp. (FE) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Brian X. Tierney, CEO, opened by highlighting, "GAAP earnings for the second quarter were $0.46 per share compared to $0.08 in the second quarter of 2024. Core earnings were $0.52 per share for the quarter compared to $0.51 in the second quarter of last year. We are on track to deliver results in the upper half of our full year 2025 guidance range of $2.40 to $2.60 per share." He emphasized strong execution on investment strategy, new base rates in Pennsylvania, and increased transmission investments benefiting from formula-based rates.
  • Tierney reported, "Through the first 6 months of 2025, we invested $2.5 billion in our infrastructure through Energize 365. We are on pace to deploy $5 billion in capital this year, and we have confidence in our $28 billion capital investment plan through 2029." He also noted, "Our Pennsylvania capital investment plans are designed to improve reliability and resiliency and drive economic development."
  • Notably, Tierney disclosed, "Since February of this year, our long-term pipeline for data center load has increased over 80% to 11.1 gigawatts from 6.1 gigawatts. Our contracted data center load through 2029 has increased approximately 25% since February of this year to 2.7 gigawatts from 2.2 gigawatts."
  • He stated, "Organic investments in our transmission system are expected to drive rate base growth at a 15% compound annual growth rate between now and 2029." Tierney added, "We expect transmission investment to increase up to 20% in our next 5-year plan."
  • On regulatory updates, Tierney explained, "We are also preparing for the upcoming transition to Ohio's new regulatory framework, which includes multiyear rate cases and forward test years." In West Virginia, the company is preparing to file its 10-year integrated resource plan by October 1, with expectations that "the IRP will highlight the need for new dispatchable generation in the state."
  • CFO K. Jon Taylor commented, "Through the first half, we have delivered on each of our key financial metrics, including core earnings, capital investments, base O&M, which reflects discipline with our operating expenses, and cash from operations, our low-cost funding source for capital allocation."
  • Taylor announced, "Through June 30, our cash from operations was $1.7 billion, an increase of 60% as compared to 2024."
  • Taylor also confirmed the company "successfully sold our minority ownership position in the Signal Peak coal mine earlier this month for $47.5 million. This is a full exit, and we have no remaining financial or operational liability."

Outlook

  • Tierney reaffirmed, "We are on track to deliver results in the upper half of our full year 2025 guidance range of $2.40 to $2.60 per share. We are also reiterating our 5-year $28 billion base capital investment program with no incremental equity needs in the plan."
  • The company is targeting a "compound annual growth rate of 6% to 8% through 2029."
  • Management sees customer-focused investments driving a "targeted shareholder return opportunity of 10% to 12% with upside potential."
  • Taylor expressed ongoing commitment to "identifying sustainable solutions in our cost structure that offset inflation as well as building in flexibility to our financial plans as needed."

Financial Results

  • Second quarter core earnings were $0.52 per share, up from $0.51 in the same period last year. GAAP earnings reached $0.46 per share compared to $0.08 year-over-year.
  • Through the first half of 2025, core earnings totaled $1.19 per share, reflecting 19% growth versus the first half of 2024.
  • Year-to-date O&M expenses are stated to be "lower than planned by nearly 4%, and we expect to continue this trend through the balance of the year," according to Taylor.
  • Cash from operations for the first half was $1.7 billion, an increase of 60% compared to last year.
  • The company completed six subsidiary debt transactions totaling $1.6 billion and executed a $2.5 billion convertible debt offering in June.

Q&A

  • Nicholas Joseph Campanella, Barclays: Asked for clarification on the 20% increase in transmission CapEx and how much is upside to the five-year plan. Taylor responded, "we show our CapEx gross. So in the $28 billion, that $14 billion is our consolidated CapEx. So we -- that 20% would be on that same basis." Tierney added, "incrementally, it could be $2.3 billion to almost $4 billion."
  • Campanella followed on balance sheet capacity and equity needs. Tierney said, "We look at that all the time, Nick. ... Not very concerned about it at all."
  • Jeremy Bryan Tonet, JPMorgan: Inquired about drivers for data center pipeline growth and obstacles. Tierney replied, "It's really customer demand and how quickly people are willing to put their dollars to work."
  • Tonet also asked about incremental generation in West Virginia. Tierney stated, "I could see us incrementally adding over a period of time, 1,000 megawatts of dispatchable gas combined cycle over the next 10 years."
  • Carly S. Davenport, Goldman Sachs: Probed on drivers of upside vs. plan and guidance range adjustment. Taylor explained, "most of the favorability to the plan year-to-date is just discipline around operating expenses."
  • David Keith Arcaro, Morgan Stanley: Asked about further upside to CapEx outlook. Tierney responded, "I do think there will be upside as we go forward in time."
  • Ross Allen Fowler, Bank of America: Discussed Ohio regulation and the new forward test year, with Tierney stating, "I think the new regime will be constructive and look forward to transitioning to that as quickly as possible."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on clarity around CapEx growth, regulatory developments, and drivers of upside, generally expressing neutral to slightly positive sentiment, with questions seeking detail and validation rather than skepticism.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing financial discipline, growth opportunities, and constructive regulatory relationships, as evidenced by phrases such as "we are on track" and "we are confident."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone remains optimistic, but with increased focus on transmission investment, data center demand, and execution of capital plans.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw a significant increase in data center pipeline and contracted load, with an 80% rise in long-term data center load pipeline since February and a 25% increase in contracted data center load.
  • Guidance language is more assertive, with management reaffirming its upper-half guidance target and projecting up to a 20% increase in transmission capital investment.
  • Analysts' questions continue to focus on regulatory progress, CapEx upside, and execution, though with heightened attention to transmission growth and data center trends.
  • Management's confidence level is steady, with both quarters highlighting financial discipline and shareholder value, but the latest call places stronger emphasis on the potential for incremental investment and expansion.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management indicated ongoing regulatory processes in Ohio and the outcome of the current base rate case as areas of focus.
  • Severe weather and system strain were noted, with Tierney stating, "severe weather strained the system in several of our locations. We are committed to resolving these issues quickly and to making the long-term investments to prevent outages before they happen."
  • Industry-wide transmission CapEx raises concerns about equipment procurement, but Tierney reassured, "We're confident that we have the relationships with vendors and suppliers ... that we will be able to deliver against our commitments."
  • The need for new dispatchable generation in West Virginia is highlighted, with the upcoming IRP expected to address these requirements.

Final Takeaway

FirstEnergy’s second quarter call underscored the company’s momentum in executing its investment plan, with a robust expansion in data center-related demand driving new opportunities for transmission growth. Management affirmed guidance in the upper half of the $2.40 to $2.60 per share range, detailed disciplined cost management, and highlighted confidence in meeting capital deployment goals without incremental equity. The company sees continued regulatory engagement, a healthy balance sheet, and a $28 billion capital plan through 2029 as key elements supporting sustainable growth and shareholder returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Utz signals EBITDA margin expansion and revised EPS guidance amid infrastructure investments
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Utz Brands, Inc. (UTZ) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Howard A. Friedman reported confidence in the company's full-year EBITDA outlook, stating that investments made to drive top-line growth are “coming through” and highlighted that “Q3 is typically a significant step up versus Q2.” Friedman emphasized accelerated capital expenditure (CapEx) supporting productivity savings, a recently announced plant closure impacting the back half of the year, and a positive portfolio mix. He added, “we feel like it's -- we're in a good place on the guide and have some clarity and line of sight to the productivity savings and the margin expansion as we progress through the rest of the year.”
  • Friedman indicated that distribution gains, improved C-store performance, and investment in geographic expansion are supporting strong top-line results, as “measured and unmeasured channels, all kind of moving together very positively.”
  • On the revision to EPS guidance, CFO William J. Kelley said, “The original guide that we put in place was the EPS growth of 10% to 15%. We revised that in our print yesterday to be -- or this morning to be 7% to 10%.” Kelley attributed this to higher CapEx spending, increased cash interest, and higher depreciation and amortization, explaining, “On the interest side...we borrowed a bit more sooner...that's picking up there in terms of more interest. On the CapEx side, we did confirm a higher spend on the -- higher end of our range on CapEx and we spent more of that more quickly than in the past.” Kelley also stated, “we are confident that 2025 will be the peak of our CapEx spending.”

Outlook

  • Management maintained confidence in achieving full-year EBITDA growth, with productivity savings and margin expansion expected to accelerate in the second half of the year. Friedman said, “we obviously nudged up the low end from 6 to 10 to 7 to 10.”
  • EPS growth guidance was revised to 7% to 10%, down from the previous 10% to 15%, due to increased interest and depreciation from accelerated CapEx. Kelley noted, “the impact of our revision, the midpoint to midpoint is about $0.03. Half of that is interest. The other half is D&A.”
  • Management stated that 2025 will be the peak for CapEx spending.

Financial Results

  • Management referenced strong top-line momentum in the first half, with distribution gains in both core and expansion geographies, supported by increased infrastructure and marketing investments. Friedman stated, “distribution gains supporting it. And then we've been making investments to drive that westward expansion, and they're also coming through the top line results.”
  • EBITDA was reported as “roughly flat in the first half of the year,” but management expects “high-teens growth in the back half.”
  • The company reported that CapEx is “about 70% spent through the first half of the year.”

Q&A

  • Andrew Lazar, Barclays: Asked about the company’s confidence in achieving 8.5% EBITDA growth for the year after a muted first half. Friedman cited CapEx-driven productivity, plant closures, and favorable portfolio mix as drivers for back-half improvement.
  • Peter Galbo, BofA Securities: Sought clarity on EPS guidance revision. Kelley detailed the shift, attributing it to “higher CapEx spend...and more amortization and depreciation.”
  • Michael Lavery, Piper Sandler: Inquired about distribution gains, particularly in the Midwest. Friedman explained that gains are broad-based, with “very strong retailer support from national chains” and momentum in club, discount, and dollar channels.
  • Robert Moskow, TD Cowen: Asked if elevated SG&A investment will continue. Friedman confirmed second-half SG&A will be “modestly higher” as infrastructure and marketing spend persists, supporting top-line momentum.
  • Brian Holland, D.A. Davidson: Queried about promotional intensity. Friedman noted the category is “fairly stable” and that the company’s guide assumes no significant improvement in the category.
  • John Baumgartner, Mizuho: Asked about DSD investments and C-store performance. Friedman described ongoing infrastructure investment and route optimization, with C-store momentum improving and expected to be “flattish by the end of the year.”
  • Scott Marks, Jefferies: Asked about supply chain and marketing strategy. Friedman and Kelley highlighted progress in manufacturing optimization, automation, and increased marketing spend, stating, “our automation is now in place and is now active.”
  • Rupesh Parikh, Oppenheimer: Asked about top-line drivers and value proposition. Friedman cited expanded distribution, in-store support, and infrastructure investment as key drivers, with the value proposition remaining unchanged despite the conclusion of bonus packs.
  • Peter Grom, UBS: Inquired about long-term category growth and protein innovation. Friedman expressed bullishness on the category and acknowledged ongoing interest in protein-based snack innovation.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concern about the EPS guidance revision and sought reassurance on back-half EBITDA acceleration and strategic investments, with several referencing the stock’s negative reaction during the call. The tone was slightly negative, particularly around the EPS downgrade and CapEx spend.
  • Management maintained a confident tone on productivity, margin expansion, and strategic execution, though more defensive and explanatory when discussing EPS guidance changes. Friedman repeatedly emphasized clarity and confidence in EBITDA growth and productivity savings. The sentiment was positive on business fundamentals, slightly defensive when addressing financial guidance revisions.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed more urgency and scrutiny regarding guidance and capital deployment, with management presenting more detailed justifications for strategic investments and EPS guidance changes.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter included a downward revision to EPS guidance (now 7%-10% growth), contrasted with the prior quarter’s 10%-15% range. EBITDA outlook was reaffirmed with a slight increase in the lower bound, from 6%-10% to 7%-10%.
  • Strategic focus shifted further toward infrastructure and CapEx acceleration, with management emphasizing near-term margin expansion and long-term productivity. Previous quarter’s focus was on distribution gains and bonus pack program; current quarter highlights infrastructure, plant closures, and automation.
  • Analysts shifted from questions about market expansion and product performance to more pointed inquiries about financial mechanics, guidance revisions, and capital allocation.
  • Both quarters emphasized Boulder Canyon’s performance and geographic expansion, but current management tone was more cautious and detailed in financial explanations.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited increased interest expense and accelerated depreciation as factors impacting EPS.
  • CapEx spending is at a historical peak, with 70% deployed in the first half, raising questions about balance sheet impact and return on investment.
  • Analysts raised concerns over promotional intensity, category stagnation, and the sustainability of top-line momentum as the category remains “fairly stable.”
  • Management acknowledged ongoing risk from supply chain optimization and plant closures but expressed confidence these actions would drive margin expansion.

Final Takeaway

Utz Brands management emphasized continued progress in productivity and geographic expansion, while acknowledging higher CapEx and related impacts on EPS guidance. The company remains confident in delivering EBITDA margin expansion through the second half of the year, supported by infrastructure investments, automation, and a strong performance from brands such as Boulder Canyon. While short-term earnings guidance has been revised downward due to higher interest and depreciation, management maintains a positive outlook on long-term value creation and operational execution.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Builders FirstSource outlines $14.8B–$15.6B sales guidance as market conditions remain challenging
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Builders FirstSource (BLDR) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Peter M. Jackson stated the company is "prioritizing what's within our control; serving customers with excellence, leveraging technology and managing the business with discipline" to strengthen its position amid a challenging market. He emphasized continued expansion of value-added solutions and technology, referencing over $35 million invested in value-added solutions in Q2, including a new millwork facility in Florida and plant upgrades in seven states.
  • Jackson reported, "We generated $5 million in productivity savings in Q2, primarily through targeted supply chain initiatives," and highlighted an on-time and in-full delivery rate of 92%, even as eight facilities were consolidated year-to-date.
  • The company acquired Truckee-Tahoe Lumber, extending its reach in Northern California and Nevada, and deployed over $500 million toward capital priorities, including organic growth, M&A, and share repurchases in the quarter.
  • Jackson announced the appointment of Gayatri Narayan as President of Technology and Digital Solutions, citing her "over 2 decades of global technology leadership experience." He noted, "Since launch in early 2024, we have seen more than $2 billion of orders and $4 billion of quotes placed through our BFS digital tools. These metrics are up more than 400% and nearly 300% year-to-date, respectively, compared to 2024."
  • CFO Pete R. Beckmann stated, "Net sales decreased 5%, $4.2 billion, driven by lower organic sales and commodity deflation, partially offset by growth from acquisitions." Beckmann explained, "Adjusted EBITDA was $506 million, down 24%, primarily driven by lower gross profit... Adjusted EPS was $2.38, a decrease of 32% compared to the prior year."

Outlook

  • Beckmann provided updated 2025 guidance: "We are guiding net sales in the range of $14.8 billion to $15.6 billion. We expect adjusted EBITDA to be $1.5 billion to $1.7 billion. Adjusted EBITDA margin is forecast to be in the range of 10.1% to 10.9%."
  • He added, "We expect Q3 net sales to be between $3.65 and $3.95 billion given a weaker-than-normal building season. Q3 adjusted EBITDA is expected to be between $375 million and $425 million."
  • The company revised its gross margin outlook: "We expect our 2025 full year gross margin to be below long-term normalized levels and in a range of 29% to 30.5%. We expect free cash flow of $800 million to $1 billion."
  • Jackson commented, "Our latest forecast assumes Single-Family is down 10% to 12% for the year. We continue to expect a multi-family headwind to sales of $400 million to $500 million and a headwind to EBITDA of less than $200 million, with most of the drag already coming in the first half of the year."

Financial Results

  • Beckmann detailed, "Gross profit was $1.3 billion, a decrease of 11% compared to the prior year period. Gross margin was 30.7%, down 210 basis points. Adjusted SG&A of $818 million increased $4 million, primarily attributable to acquired operations."
  • Operating cash flow was $341 million, a decrease of $111 million, mainly attributable to lower net income; free cash flow was $255 million. The company reported a net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of approximately 2.3x and completed a $750 million senior unsecured notes offering.
  • Capital expenditures were $86 million; $61 million was deployed on acquisitions, and $391 million spent on repurchasing 3.3 million shares.

Q&A

  • Matthew Adrien Bouley, Barclays: Asked about competitive positioning and margin drivers. Jackson responded, "A big piece of that is around technology, both in the core systems, but as well as digital... Digital is another way where we have seen customers benefit in terms of utilizing both online tools, but also the 3-dimensional digital twin to optimize their build process."
  • Bouley also questioned Q2 gross margins. Beckmann said, "We were very pleased with that margin performance for Q2... that 20 basis points increase over Q1, that's worth about $8 million."
  • Michael Glaser Dahl, RBC: Probed on margin guidance and market dynamics. Jackson replied, "The competitive environment... it's pretty stable, it's a tough market. There's a fight out there in terms of what we're seeing when we're quoting and looking at what's going on with share according to our calcs. We think things are pretty well stabilized on the share side."
  • Dahl asked about truss capacity and margins. Jackson explained, "We're underutilized from a capacity perspective and trust, given where starts are... we mothball operations or close operations or consolidate in order to ensure that the remaining facilities do have enough to stay stable."
  • John Lovallo, UBS: Asked about Canadian lumber tariffs. Beckmann stated, "We have factored in the duties and increases into our guidance for the lumber side... I don't think will hit us really in our numbers for at least 3 to 4 months."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was slightly negative, focusing on concerns around margin pressure, capacity utilization, and the impact of tariffs. Questions repeatedly circled around market share, profitability, and risk to guidance, with pressing requests for clarity on headwinds and cost controls.
  • Management maintained a neutral-to-slightly positive tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing adaptability and operational discipline. However, during Q&A, responses occasionally shifted toward a more cautious or defensive posture, as seen in phrases such as "we're very confident that we are as efficient or better than anybody else" and acknowledgment that "it's a competitive environment."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management exhibited increased concern about the pace of market recovery and margin sustainability, though management continued to stress readiness for eventual market improvement.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2025 net sales was revised downward from $16.05–$17.05 billion last quarter to $14.8–$15.6 billion, reflecting weaker expectations for single-family starts and commodity prices.
  • The EBITDA guidance range narrowed and moved lower, and full-year gross margin expectations were trimmed below long-term normalized levels.
  • The strategic emphasis on digital adoption and operational efficiency was reiterated, but management noted a slower-than-expected pace of digital tool adoption.
  • Analysts this quarter focused even more on cost discipline, downside scenarios, and mitigation plans for persistent volume softness.
  • Management's tone shifted from resilient optimism last quarter to a more measured and risk-aware approach, frequently referencing the challenging market environment.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited persistent softness in single-family and multi-family starts, ongoing affordability issues, and the risk of further commodity price declines—especially for OSB—as challenges.
  • The company is consolidating facilities and managing headcount to align capacity with demand, and closely monitoring discretionary spend.
  • Analysts expressed concern over capacity utilization, the impact of tariffs, and the sustainability of margins in a low-volume environment.
  • The pace of M&A was described as slow due to market volatility and price discovery challenges, though management expects inorganic investments to be important longer-term.

Final Takeaway

Builders FirstSource leadership emphasized its disciplined strategy and investment in digital tools and operational efficiency to navigate a slower housing market. Updated guidance reflects ongoing headwinds in single-family and multi-family demand, with cost controls and capital allocation remaining central priorities. Management highlighted that its operational improvements, technology investments, and prudent capital deployment position the company to accelerate growth as market conditions improve, while remaining committed to maximizing shareholder value through the current downcycle.
